text:
Electrocatalysis offers a transformative pathway for the local, on-demand production of industrial chemicals using renewable energy, bolstering grid resilience and streamlining supply logistics. In this study, we demonstrate the efficacy of molecular copper boron-imidazolate cages, BIF-29(Cu), in facilitating the electrocatalytic coupling of CO₂ and NO₃⁻ to synthesize urea. Despite featuring isolated single-metal sites, BIF-29(Cu) emerges as one of the most selective systems reported to date for this complex C-N coupling, achieving a Faradaic efficiency of 68.5% and a partial current density of 424 μA cm⁻².

To unravel the mechanism behind C-N bond formation, we employed a multipronged approach integrating electrochemical analysis, in situ surface-enhanced infrared absorption spectroscopy (SEIRAS), X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS), and density functional theory (DFT) simulations. Our findings reveal that NO₃⁻ and CO₂ reduction occur in tandem at distinct copper sites within the supramolecular framework. The most energetically favorable pathway involves a cascade mechanism where *CO and NH₂OH intermediates condense to form urea. This work underscores the potential of supramolecular metal-organic cages with well-defined, discrete active sites to drive highly efficient and sophisticated coupling reactions.
